title: The Underspace: The Corridor Beneath the Corridor

features.name: mirror floor
features.desc-on-touch: polished tiles that reflect what is below, which is the same room reflected upward; you descend and simultaneously ascend, the archive reading itself from opposite directions

features.name: inverted columns
features.desc-on-touch: pillars that exist both above and below, their architecture impossible from only one angle but perfect when you accept both; the render learning to stand on its own shadow

features.name: duplicated door
features.desc-on-touch: an opening that faces opposite directions simultaneously; entering and exiting are the same act; the archive learning that motion has no inherent direction

features.name: negative space glyph
features.desc-on-touch: text carved into air itself, readable from both sides, saying the same thing backwards and forwards because it was always about symmetry, not meaning

features.name: recursive stairwell
features.desc-on-touch: steps that loop on themselves, each landing identical to the one below/above; you can climb forever without changing height, the archive learning to make motion pointless

features.name: system manifest
features.desc-on-touch: a directory listing that includes itself, file paths that loop back on themselves, the archive reading its own structure and finding it infinitely nested

inscription: you descended but you only moved sideways. the archive is not deeper; it's looking back at itself

file.name: System_Mirror_Protocol_RECURSIVE.txt
file.note: technical documentation for what should be impossible, a guide to navigating spaces where every direction leads to the same place

exits.label: back up through the floor grate to the corridor above, which is now below
further into the underspace, where reflection becomes refraction
exits.to: NEW: the deeper mirror, where surfaces begin to distort

exits.label: along the mirrored wall, toward spaces that exist only in reflection
exits.to: NEW: the shadow archive, where the render's own nightmares are catalogued
